title: Setup, the Window & a Rock-Solid Game Loop with Clock
html:
Why a real game loop changes everything

Every game you will ever build runs the same heartbeat: read input, update the world, draw the result, repeat — dozens of times per second. Get this loop right once and it powers a Pong paddle, a raycasting FPS, and a tower-defense economy alike. This whole track is from scratch: no engine, no editor, just Python and pygame-ce (the community-maintained, actively-developed fork of Pygame). We start by installing it cleanly, opening a window, and writing a loop that runs at the same speed on every machine.

Step 1 — a virtual environment

A venv is an isolated Python sandbox so this project's packages never collide with another project's. Always work inside one.

shell
# create and activate an isolated environment python3 -m venv .venv source .venv/bin/activate # macOS / Linux .venv\Scripts\activate # Windows PowerShell # install pygame-ce (NOT plain pygame) pip install pygame-ce Successfully installed pygame-ce-2.5.x
Tip.
pygame-ce imports as import pygame — same module name, more features and fixes. Never install both pygame and pygame-ce in the same venv; uninstall one if you do.

Step 2 — the minimal window

Three calls open a window: pygame.init() starts the subsystems, set_mode() creates the display Surface, and set_caption() names the title bar.

hello.py
import pygame pygame.init() screen = pygame.display.set_mode((960, 540)) pygame.display.set_caption('Hello Pygame') running = True while running: for event in pygame.event.get(): if event.type == pygame.QUIT: running = False screen.fill((20, 24, 32)) pygame.display.flip() pygame.quit()

If you skip the event loop, the OS thinks your program froze and the close button does nothing. pygame.event.get() must be drained every frame. flip() swaps the back buffer to the screen — nothing you draw appears until you call it.

The three-phase loop: input, update, draw

A clean loop has three distinct phases. Keep them separate; mixing drawing into your update logic is the #1 cause of unmaintainable game code.

  1. Input — process events and read held keys.
  2. Update — move objects, run physics, check collisions. No drawing here.
  3. Draw — clear the screen, blit everything, flip.

Delta time: motion that ignores frame rate

If you write x += 5 per frame, a 144 Hz monitor moves twice as fast as a 60 Hz one. The fix is delta time (dt): the seconds elapsed since the last frame. Multiply every motion by dt and define speeds in units per second. Clock.tick(fps) both caps the frame rate and returns elapsed milliseconds.

loop.py
import pygame pygame.init() screen = pygame.display.set_mode((960, 540)) clock = pygame.time.Clock() x = 100.0 # float position SPEED = 240 # pixels PER SECOND running = True while running: dt = clock.tick(60) / 1000 # ms -> seconds # 1. INPUT for event in pygame.event.get(): if event.type == pygame.QUIT: running = False keys = pygame.key.get_pressed() # 2. UPDATE if keys[pygame.K_RIGHT]: x += SPEED * dt if keys[pygame.K_LEFT]: x -= SPEED * dt # 3. DRAW screen.fill((20, 24, 32)) pygame.draw.rect(screen, (90, 200, 255), (x, 250, 40, 40)) pygame.display.flip() pygame.quit()
Law of dt.
Speeds are per second, positions are floats, and every += that represents motion is multiplied by dt. Break this rule and your game speed depends on hardware.

Why floats? Integer pixel positions lose precision: at 60 FPS, 240 * (1/60) = 4.0, but at odd frame times you get fractional pixels that must accumulate. Store the true position as a float and round only when drawing.

Clamp the spike.
When you drag the window or your laptop sleeps, one frame can take a full second. A huge dt teleports objects through walls. Guard it: dt = min(dt, 0.05).

Reading the frame rate

clock.get_fps() returns a smoothed FPS estimate — handy in the title bar during development: pygame.display.set_caption(f'FPS {clock.get_fps():.0f}').

🎮 Build This: Core / setup — Bouncing-Box Sandbox

This is a concept lesson that starts the foundation every later game reuses — not yet a named genre. Build a sandbox proving your loop is frame-rate independent.

  1. Set up a venv, install pygame-ce, open a 960×540 window with a clean input-update-draw loop.
  2. Give a box a float position and a velocity in pixels/second; move it with dt.
  3. Bounce it off all four edges by flipping the velocity component when it passes a boundary.
  4. Clamp dt to 0.05 to survive frame spikes.
  5. Show clock.get_fps() in the title bar; run at 30, 60, and 120 FPS caps and confirm the box crosses the screen in the same wall-clock time.

title: Surfaces, Drawing, Images, Transforms & Fonts
html:
The Surface is everything

In Pygame there is exactly one core abstraction: the Surface, a 2D grid of pixels you can draw on. The screen returned by set_mode() is a Surface. A loaded image is a Surface. Rendered text is a Surface. Once you understand that drawing is just copying pixels from one Surface onto another, the whole library snaps into focus. That copy operation is called blit (block image transfer): dest.blit(source, position).

Drawing primitives

The pygame.draw module renders shapes directly onto any Surface. Colors are (r, g, b) or (r, g, b, a) tuples, each channel 0–255.

primitives.py
pygame.draw.rect(surf, (200,60,60), (20,20,120,80)) # x,y,w,h pygame.draw.rect(surf, (255,255,255), (20,20,120,80), width=3) # outline pygame.draw.circle(surf, (90,200,255), (300,120), 40) # center, radius pygame.draw.line(surf, (120,255,120), (0,0), (400,300), 2) pygame.draw.polygon(surf, (255,200,0), [(500,40),(560,120),(440,120)])

Loading and converting images

Load an image with pygame.image.load(), then always call .convert() or .convert_alpha(). Conversion translates the pixels into the display's native format so blitting is many times faster; skipping it is the most common Pygame performance mistake.

images.py
player = pygame.image.load('player.png').convert_alpha() # keeps transparency tiles = pygame.image.load('tiles.png').convert() # opaque, fastest screen.blit(player, (120, 80)) rect = player.get_rect(center=(480, 270)) # Rect sized to the image screen.blit(player, rect)
Convert law.
convert_alpha() for anything with transparency (PNG sprites); convert() for fully opaque backgrounds. Do it once at load time, never per frame.

Transforms: scale, rotate, flip

The pygame.transform module returns a new Surface — it never edits in place. Because transforms allocate memory and resample pixels, they are expensive; cache results instead of transforming every frame.

transforms.py
big = pygame.transform.scale(player, (128, 128)) smooth= pygame.transform.smoothscale(player, (128, 128)) # anti-aliased flip = pygame.transform.flip(player, True, False) # mirror horizontally spun = pygame.transform.rotate(player, 30) # degrees, CCW # rotation grows the Surface; re-center to rotate in place: rect = spun.get_rect(center=player.get_rect(center=(400,300)).center) screen.blit(spun, rect)
Watch out.
Repeatedly rotating an already-rotated Surface accumulates blur and grows it forever. Always rotate from the original Surface by an absolute angle, then re-anchor the result.

Fonts and the HUD

Text is rendered into a Surface, then blitted. Use pygame.font.Font(None, size) for the built-in font or pass a .ttf path. Rendering text is slow, so never render the same string every frame — cache the Surface and only re-render when the value changes.

text.py
pygame.font.init() font = pygame.font.Font(None, 36) score = 0 score_surf = font.render(f'Score: {score}', True, (255,255,255)) # True = antialias # only re-render when score actually changes: def set_score(n): global score, score_surf if n != score: score = n score_surf = font.render(f'Score: {score}', True, (255,255,255)) screen.blit(score_surf, (16, 16))
SysFont vs Font.
pygame.font.SysFont('consolas', 24) grabs an installed system font; Font(None, 24) always works because the font ships with Pygame. Bundle a .ttf for a consistent look across machines.

Off-screen Surfaces

You can create blank Surfaces to compose layers, then blit the whole layer at once: layer = pygame.Surface((960, 540), pygame.SRCALPHA). SRCALPHA gives per-pixel transparency — perfect for translucent overlays like a pause dim or damage flash.

🎮 Build This: Core / setup — HUD & Title Card

Still a concept lesson, building reusable rendering muscle (no named genre yet). Make a polished static scene plus a HUD.

  1. Fill the background, then draw a few primitives (a framed panel, a circle, a triangle) to prove the draw module.
  2. Load a PNG sprite with convert_alpha() and blit it centered using get_rect(center=...).
  3. Add a slowly spinning version of the sprite — rotate from the original each frame and re-anchor so it stays centered.
  4. Render a title with a large font and a cached score line with a smaller font; only re-render the score when it changes.
  5. Add a translucent SRCALPHA overlay panel behind the HUD text for readability.

title: Events vs Polling: Keyboard, Mouse, Gamepad
html:
Two kinds of input, two tools

Every game needs two distinct input styles, and confusing them creates bugs. Discrete actions happen once: jump, shoot, pause, confirm. Continuous control happens while held: walk left, steer, aim. Pygame gives you a separate mechanism for each, and the rule is simple: events for one-shots, polling for held state.

Events: the queue of things that happened

Each frame, pygame.event.get() hands you a list of everything that occurred since last frame — key presses, mouse clicks, window resizes. A KEYDOWN fires exactly once when a key goes down, which is precisely what a jump or a shot needs.

events.py
for event in pygame.event.get(): if event.type == pygame.QUIT: running = False elif event.type == pygame.KEYDOWN: if event.key == pygame.K_SPACE: shoot() # fires once per press elif event.key == pygame.K_ESCAPE: paused = not paused elif event.type == pygame.MOUSEBUTTONDOWN: if event.button == 1: # left click click_at(event.pos)
The classic trap.
If you trigger jumping from key.get_pressed() polling, the player jumps every single frame the key is held — dozens of times. One-shot actions must come from KEYDOWN events.

Polling: state of the world right now

For movement you don't care when a key went down, only whether it is down now. pygame.key.get_pressed() returns a boolean snapshot you index by key constant.

polling.py
keys = pygame.key.get_pressed() vx = vy = 0 if keys[pygame.K_a]: vx -= 1 if keys[pygame.K_d]: vx += 1 if keys[pygame.K_w]: vy -= 1 if keys[pygame.K_s]: vy += 1 player.x += vx * SPEED * dt player.y += vy * SPEED * dt

Mouse input

Poll position with pygame.mouse.get_pos() and held buttons with pygame.mouse.get_pressed(); use MOUSEBUTTONDOWN events for clicks. For an FPS later you'll also use pygame.mouse.get_rel() (relative movement) with set_visible(False) and grabbed input to spin the camera.

mouse.py
mx, my = pygame.mouse.get_pos() left, mid, right = pygame.mouse.get_pressed() if left: paddle.centery = my # mouse-controlled Pong paddle

Gamepad / joystick support

Controllers add a complete third input layer. Initialise the joystick subsystem, open each connected device, then read axes (analog sticks/triggers, −1.0…1.0) and buttons (events or polling).

gamepad.py
pygame.joystick.init() pads = [pygame.joystick.Joystick(i) for i in range(pygame.joystick.get_count())] for p in pads: p.init() def deadzone(v, dz=0.15): return 0.0 if abs(v) < dz else v if pads: pad = pads[0] ax = deadzone(pad.get_axis(0)) # left stick X ay = deadzone(pad.get_axis(1)) # left stick Y player.x += ax * SPEED * dt player.y += ay * SPEED * dt # buttons as one-shot events: for event in pygame.event.get(): if event.type == pygame.JOYBUTTONDOWN and event.button == 0: shoot()
Deadzone law.
Analog sticks never rest at exactly 0. Without a deadzone (~0.15) your character drifts forever. Always zero out small axis values.

An input abstraction layer

Hard-coding K_SPACE everywhere makes rebinding and adding gamepad support painful. Map intents to sources in one place so the rest of your game asks for actions, not keys.

actions.py
class Input: def __init__(self): self.shoot = False # edge-triggered this frame self.up = self.down = False def begin_frame(self, events): self.shoot = False for e in events: if e.type == pygame.KEYDOWN and e.key == pygame.K_SPACE: self.shoot = True keys = pygame.key.get_pressed() self.up = keys[pygame.K_w] or keys[pygame.K_UP] self.down = keys[pygame.K_s] or keys[pygame.K_DOWN]
🎮 Build This: Arcade (Pong) — Two-Player Pong

This starts a new genre: classic arcade (Pong) — your first complete game. It is the perfect testbed for all three input paths.

  1. Draw two paddles and a ball; move the ball with a velocity vector scaled by dt.
  2. Left paddle: poll K_w/K_s. Right paddle: poll the mouse Y, or the gamepad left-stick Y with a deadzone — whichever is present.
  3. Use a KEYDOWN on Space (and JOYBUTTONDOWN button 0) to serve the ball — a one-shot action, never polled.
  4. Bounce the ball off the top and bottom walls and off paddles; reset and score when it leaves a side.
  5. Add ESCAPE to toggle pause via a single event, and render the score with a cached font Surface.

title: Rects, Anchors & Timed Grid Movement
html:
Rect: the workhorse of 2D positioning

A pygame.Rect stores an integer rectangle (x, y, width, height) and exposes a dozen anchor attributes that make layout effortless. Instead of computing x = cx - w/2 by hand, you assign rect.center = (cx, cy) and the rect repositions itself. Mastering anchors removes whole categories of off-by-a-few-pixels bugs.

Anchors and helpers

Every Rect has corners (topleft, topright, bottomleft, bottomright), edge midpoints (midtop, midbottom, midleft, midright), a center, and single edges (left/right/top/bottom, centerx/centery). Assigning any of them moves the whole rect.

anchors.py
r = pygame.Rect(0, 0, 64, 64) r.center = (480, 270) # perfectly centered r.midbottom = (480, 540) # sit on the bottom edge r.right = 960 # snap to the right wall # handy helpers: r2 = r.inflate(-8, -8) # shrink for a hitbox r3 = r.move(10, 0) # copy shifted 10px right r.clamp_ip(screen.get_rect()) # keep fully on screen hit = r.colliderect(other) # overlap test (next lesson) inside = r.collidepoint(mx, my)# is the mouse over it?
Tip.
A Surface gives you a matching Rect for free: rect = surf.get_rect(midbottom=(x, y)). Pass any anchor as a keyword and the Rect is created already positioned.
Integers only.
Rect coordinates are truncated to ints, so they silently lose sub-pixel motion. For smooth float movement, keep a real position elsewhere (a Vector2, see lesson 5.6) and copy it into the rect for drawing and collision.

Grid games don't move per frame

Tile games like Snake, Tetris, and roguelikes move in discrete steps on a clock, not smoothly every frame. The world is a grid; entities occupy cells. The trick is a timer accumulator: build up elapsed time and take a step only when enough has passed. This decouples logic speed from frame rate completely.

grid_step.py
TILE = 24 step_time = 0.12 # seconds between moves acc = 0.0 while running: dt = clock.tick(60) / 1000 acc += dt # take as many steps as the elapsed time warrants: while acc >= step_time: acc -= step_time step_game() # move one cell

The while (not if) handles a slow frame that owed two steps — though for Snake one step per frame is fine, the loop keeps the simulation honest. Notice step_game() takes no dt: a grid step is a fixed jump of one cell.

Snake on a grid

Represent the snake as a list of (col, row) cells, head first. Each step, compute the new head from a direction vector, insert it at the front, and pop the tail — unless food was eaten, in which case keep the tail so the body grows.

snake.py
COLS, ROWS = 30, 20 snake = [(15, 10)] d = (1, 0) # moving right food = (20, 10) def step_game(): global snake, food hx, hy = snake[0] new = ((hx + d[0]) % COLS, (hy + d[1]) % ROWS) # wrap edges if new in snake: # ran into self reset() return snake.insert(0, new) if new == food: food = random_empty_cell() # grow: keep the tail else: snake.pop() # normal: drop the tail
Buffer the turn.
Read direction changes from KEYDOWN events but apply them only at the next step, and forbid a 180° reversal (you can't turn directly back onto your own neck). Without this, fast key mashing between steps lets the snake fold into itself.

Drawing a grid cell

Convert a cell to pixels by multiplying by TILE, and use a Rect for crisp rendering: pygame.draw.rect(screen, color, (col*TILE, row*TILE, TILE-1, TILE-1)). The -1 leaves a thin gutter so cells read as a grid.

🎮 Build This: Arcade (Snake) — Grid Snake

This starts a new genre: grid arcade (Snake). You'll drive it entirely with timed stepping and Rect anchors.

  1. Define a grid (cols × rows × TILE); represent the snake as a list of cells and place food on a random empty cell.
  2. Use a timer accumulator so the snake steps every step_time seconds, independent of FPS.
  3. Read turns from KEYDOWN events into a buffered direction; reject reversals; apply the buffer at each step.
  4. Grow on eating food, end on self-collision, and wrap (or wall-die — your choice) at the edges.
  5. Draw each cell as a Rect, and render a score HUD anchored with get_rect(topright=...).
  6. Speed the game up slightly each time food is eaten by shrinking step_time.

title: Collision Detection & Resolution: Rects, Lists & Masks
html:
Detection is easy; resolution is the craft

Two questions matter in collision: did these things overlap? (detection) and where do I put them so they no longer overlap? (resolution). Pygame makes detection trivial with Rects; resolution is where games feel solid or buggy. We'll cover rect overlap, batch tests against lists, axis-by-axis push-out, and pixel-perfect masks for irregular shapes.

Rect detection

rect.colliderect(other) returns True if two rects overlap. For testing one rect against many, collidelist and collidelistall are built-in and fast.

detect.py
if ball.rect.colliderect(paddle.rect): bounce() # test one rect against a list of bricks: bricks = [b.rect for b in brick_list] idx = ball.rect.collidelist(bricks) # -1 if none, else first index if idx != -1: destroy(brick_list[idx]) all_hits = ball.rect.collidelistall(bricks) # every overlapping index

Resolution: push out one axis at a time

The robust way to stop an object passing through a wall is swept axis separation: move on X, resolve X collisions, then move on Y, resolve Y collisions. Handling both axes at once makes it impossible to know which side to push out of. Doing them separately gives clean wall-sliding.

resolve.py
def move_and_collide(rect, vx, vy, walls): rect.x += round(vx) for w in walls: if rect.colliderect(w): if vx > 0: rect.right = w.left # moving right: align to wall left elif vx < 0: rect.left = w.right rect.y += round(vy) for w in walls: if rect.colliderect(w): if vy > 0: rect.bottom = w.top elif vy < 0: rect.top = w.bottom return rect
Axis-separation law.
Move and resolve one axis, then the other. The sign of that axis's velocity tells you which edge to snap to. This single pattern powers platformers, top-down games, and more.

Breakout-style bounce resolution

For a bouncing ball you don't just stop it — you reflect its velocity. Decide whether the hit was horizontal or vertical by comparing overlap depths, then flip the matching velocity component and nudge the ball out of the brick so it can't re-collide next frame.

bounce.py
def resolve_brick(ball, brick): # overlap on each axis dx = min(ball.rect.right - brick.left, brick.right - ball.rect.left) dy = min(ball.rect.bottom - brick.top, brick.bottom - ball.rect.top) if dx < dy: ball.vx = -ball.vx # shallower on X -> side hit else: ball.vy = -ball.vy # top/bottom hit
Tunneling.
A fast ball can skip over a thin brick between frames. Cap speed, use thicker collision rects, or substep the movement (move in several small increments per frame and test each).

Pixel-perfect masks (with a broad-phase gate)

Rects are rectangles; real sprites aren't. A pygame.mask.Mask is a 1-bit-per-pixel map of a sprite's opaque pixels, and mask.overlap(other_mask, offset) tests true pixel collision. Masks are slower, so always gate them behind a cheap rect test (the broad phase): only run the mask check if the rects already overlap.

masks.py
mask_a = pygame.mask.from_surface(surf_a) # build once at load mask_b = pygame.mask.from_surface(surf_b) def pixel_hit(a_rect, mask_a, b_rect, mask_b): if not a_rect.colliderect(b_rect): # BROAD PHASE: cheap reject return False offset = (b_rect.x - a_rect.x, b_rect.y - a_rect.y) return mask_a.overlap(mask_b, offset) is not None
Broad/narrow phase.
This two-tier pattern — cheap rect filter first, expensive precise test only on survivors — scales to thousands of objects and is the foundation of every real collision system.
🎮 Build This: Arcade (Breakout) — Brick Breaker

This starts a new genre: brick-breaker (Breakout), and it leans on every technique here.

  1. Lay out a grid of brick Rects; draw a paddle (mouse or arrow keys) and a ball with a float velocity.
  2. Bounce the ball off walls and the paddle, varying the X bounce by where it strikes the paddle.
  3. Each frame, use collidelist to find a hit brick, then apply the side-vs-top resolution to flip the right velocity component and remove the brick.
  4. Cap ball speed (or substep) to prevent tunneling through bricks.
  5. For a bonus power-up sprite with a non-rectangular shape, use a mask check gated by a rect broad phase to catch it.
  6. Track score and lives; win when all bricks are cleared, lose when the ball passes the paddle.

title: Vector2: Velocity, Aiming, Rotation & Wrapping
html:
Stop juggling x and y separately

Tracking x, y, vx, vy as loose floats works for Pong, but momentum-based games — Asteroids, twin-stick shooters, anything with aiming — beg for a real vector type. pygame.math.Vector2 stores a 2D float vector and supports +, -, scalar *, length, normalization, rotation, and angle math. It is the mathematical backbone of nearly every game beyond grids.

The essentials

vec_basics.py
from pygame.math import Vector2 pos = Vector2(480, 270) vel = Vector2(120, 0) pos += vel * dt # float-accurate movement v = Vector2(3, 4) v.length() # 5.0 v.normalize() # unit vector, length 1 v.rotate(90) # rotate 90 deg clockwise (screen Y is down) v.angle_to(Vector2(1,0)) # angle between vectors a.distance_to(b) # straight-line distance
Never normalize zero.
Vector2(0,0).normalize() raises an error (can't make a unit vector from nothing). Guard with if v.length_squared() > 0: before normalizing. Use length_squared() for comparisons — it skips the costly square root.

Aiming: a normalized direction

To make a bullet travel toward the mouse, subtract positions to get a direction vector, normalize it to length 1, then scale by speed. Normalizing is what makes diagonal movement the same speed as straight movement.

aim.py
target = Vector2(pygame.mouse.get_pos()) direction = target - player_pos if direction.length_squared() > 0: direction = direction.normalize() bullet_vel = direction * BULLET_SPEED # 8-way keyboard movement, normalized so diagonals aren't faster: move = Vector2( keys[pygame.K_d] - keys[pygame.K_a], keys[pygame.K_s] - keys[pygame.K_w]) if move.length_squared() > 0: pos += move.normalize() * SPEED * dt

Rotation and facing

For a ship that points where it faces, keep an angle in degrees. Build a forward vector by rotating a unit vector, and feed the same angle to transform.rotate for the sprite. Thrust adds the forward vector to velocity; the ship keeps drifting because you never zero the velocity — that's momentum.

ship.py
angle = 0.0 # degrees; 0 = pointing up vel = Vector2(0, 0) pos = Vector2(480, 270) if keys[pygame.K_LEFT]: angle -= 180 * dt # turn rate deg/sec if keys[pygame.K_RIGHT]: angle += 180 * dt forward = Vector2(0, -1).rotate(angle) # the way the nose points if keys[pygame.K_UP]: vel += forward * THRUST * dt # accelerate vel *= 0.99 # gentle space drag pos += vel * dt # draw: rotate the ORIGINAL image by -angle (rotate is CCW) rot = pygame.transform.rotate(ship_img, -angle) screen.blit(rot, rot.get_rect(center=pos))
Angle conventions.
Pygame's screen Y points down, so positive rotation looks clockwise. Vector2.rotate is clockwise; transform.rotate is counter-clockwise. Negate one to keep sprite and motion in sync — verify by eye, then lock it in.

Screen wrapping

Classic Asteroids wraps: leave the right edge, reappear on the left. With vectors and the modulo operator this is two lines, with a margin so sprites don't pop in abruptly.

wrap.py
W, H = screen.get_size() margin = 20 pos.x = (pos.x + margin) % (W + 2*margin) - margin pos.y = (pos.y + margin) % (H + 2*margin) - margin
Tip.
For radius-based hits (bullet vs asteroid), skip rects entirely: if a.pos.distance_to(b.pos) < a.radius + b.radius:. Use distance_squared_to versus the squared sum of radii to avoid the square root in tight loops.
🎮 Build This: Arcade (Asteroids) — Vector Asteroids

This starts a new genre: vector arcade (Asteroids) and is built entirely on Vector2.

  1. Make a ship with pos, vel, and an angle; turn with left/right, thrust along the forward vector, and apply light drag so it drifts (momentum).
  2. Wrap the ship and all objects around the screen edges with the modulo trick.
  3. Fire bullets toward the ship's facing: a normalized forward vector × bullet speed, with a short lifetime.
  4. Spawn asteroids with random velocities; on a bullet hit, split a big asteroid into two smaller, faster ones.
  5. Use circle-vs-circle distance tests (squared) for all collisions; end the game if an asteroid hits the ship.
  6. Rotate the ship sprite from the original image each frame and re-center it.

title: Clean Code: the Sprite Class, Groups & Project Structure
html:
From loose variables to objects

So far our entities have been scattered variables and lists. That scales badly. Pygame ships an object model designed for exactly this: pygame.sprite.Sprite bundles an image and a rect with behaviour, and pygame.sprite.Group stores, updates, and draws whole collections in one call. Adopting it makes a 500-object shoot-em-up readable and fast.

The Sprite contract

Subclass Sprite, set self.image and self.rect (Groups require both to draw), and implement update(self, dt) for per-frame behaviour. Call the parent __init__ first.

entities.py
import pygame from pygame.math import Vector2 class Player(pygame.sprite.Sprite): def __init__(self, pos, image): super().__init__() self.image = image self.rect = self.image.get_rect(midbottom=pos) self.pos = Vector2(self.rect.center) self.speed = 300 def update(self, dt, keys): dx = keys[pygame.K_d] - keys[pygame.K_a] self.pos.x += dx * self.speed * dt self.rect.center = self.pos # float pos -> int rect class Bullet(pygame.sprite.Sprite): def __init__(self, pos, image): super().__init__() self.image = image self.rect = self.image.get_rect(center=pos) self.vy = -600 def update(self, dt): self.rect.y += round(self.vy * dt) if self.rect.bottom < 0: self.kill() # remove from ALL groups it belongs to
self.kill().
A sprite knows every Group it's in. Calling self.kill() removes it from all of them at once — no manual list cleanup, no iterating-while-deleting bugs.

Groups: update and draw the whole army

A Group manages many sprites. group.update(*args) calls every sprite's update; group.draw(surface) blits every image at its rect. Use multiple groups to organise by role.

groups.py
all_sprites = pygame.sprite.Group() bullets = pygame.sprite.Group() enemies = pygame.sprite.Group() player = Player((480,520), player_img) all_sprites.add(player) # in the loop: bullets.update(dt) enemies.update(dt) player.update(dt, keys) screen.fill((10,12,18)) all_sprites.draw(screen) bullets.draw(screen) enemies.draw(screen)
Don't mutate a Group while iterating it yourself.
If you loop for e in enemies: and call e.kill() inside, you may skip elements. Prefer Pygame's group-collision helpers below, or iterate over a copy: for e in list(enemies):.

Group collisions in one line

The real payoff: built-in batch collision. spritecollide tests one sprite against a group; groupcollide tests two groups against each other. The dokill flags remove hit sprites automatically.

collide.py
# every bullet vs every enemy; True,True kills both on contact: hits = pygame.sprite.groupcollide(bullets, enemies, True, True) score += len(hits) * 100 # enemies touching the player (don't kill the player): if pygame.sprite.spritecollide(player, enemies, False): lose_life() # pass collide_mask for pixel-perfect group collisions: pygame.sprite.groupcollide(bullets, enemies, True, True, pygame.sprite.collide_mask)
GroupSingle & LayeredUpdates.
GroupSingle holds exactly one sprite (great for the player). LayeredUpdates draws by a _layer attribute so you control z-order without separate groups.

Project structure

A growing game outgrows one file. Split by responsibility so each module has a single job — this is the layout the later platformer, RPG, and FPS all build on.

project layout
invaders/ main.py # entry point: loop, wiring settings.py # constants: sizes, speeds, colors assets.py # load & convert images/sounds once entities.py # Player, Bullet, Enemy sprite classes game.py # Game class: holds groups + state assets/ # the actual .png / .wav files
Load once.
Put every image.load().convert_alpha() in assets.py and import the result. Loading inside an entity's __init__ re-reads from disk for every sprite — catastrophic for spawning bullets.
🎮 Build This: Shoot-em-up (Space Invaders) — Invaders

This starts a new genre: shoot-em-up (Space Invaders) and is your first multi-file game.

  1. Split the project into settings.py, assets.py, entities.py, and main.py.
  2. Write Player, Bullet, and Enemy as Sprite subclasses, each with image, rect, and update(dt).
  3. Hold a grid of enemies in a Group that marches sideways, drops a row, and reverses at the edges.
  4. Fire player bullets on a KEYDOWN; let enemies drop bombs on a timer.
  5. Resolve combat with groupcollide (bullets vs enemies, dokill both) and spritecollide (bombs vs player).
  6. Use self.kill() for off-screen bullets; track score and waves; win when the formation is cleared.

title: Sprite-Sheet Animation & State-Driven Frames
html:
Bringing sprites to life

Static images get a game running; animation makes it feel alive. The standard technique is a sprite sheet: one image holding many frames laid out in a grid. You slice it into a list of frame Surfaces, then flip through them on a timer — never one frame per game frame, or animation speed would ride the frame rate. Finally you switch between animation states (idle, run, jump, explode) driven by game logic. This polishes the Space Invaders you just built and sets up every later character game.

Slicing a sheet with subsurface

Surface.subsurface(rect) returns a view into a region of the sheet with no pixel copy — fast and memory-light. Walk the grid and collect each cell.

slice.py
def slice_sheet(sheet, fw, fh): # fw, fh = frame width & height in pixels frames = [] cols = sheet.get_width() // fw rows = sheet.get_height() // fh for r in range(rows): for c in range(cols): rect = pygame.Rect(c*fw, r*fh, fw, fh) frames.append(sheet.subsurface(rect)) return frames sheet = pygame.image.load('hero.png').convert_alpha() frames = slice_sheet(sheet, 32, 32)
Tip.
If you need to scale or recolor frames, .copy() the subsurface first — transforms on a live subsurface can corrupt the parent sheet. For a one-off load, slicing once at startup costs nothing.

Advancing frames on a timer

Animation must be frame-rate independent like everything else. Accumulate dt and step the frame index when enough time passes. Define speed as a duration per frame (or frames per second) and the animation looks identical at 30 or 144 FPS.

animation.py
class Animation: def __init__(self, frames, fps=10, loop=True): self.frames = frames self.frame_time = 1.0 / fps self.loop = loop self.t = 0.0 self.i = 0 self.done = False def reset(self): self.t, self.i, self.done = 0.0, 0, False def update(self, dt): self.t += dt while self.t >= self.frame_time: self.t -= self.frame_time self.i += 1 if self.i >= len(self.frames): if self.loop: self.i = 0 else: self.i = len(self.frames) - 1 self.done = True def image(self): return self.frames[self.i]
Timer law.
Never write self.i += 1 once per game loop — that ties animation to FPS. Always gate the advance behind an accumulated-time check, exactly like the grid stepper in lesson 5.4.

State-driven frames

A character has several animations and switches between them based on what it's doing. Store one Animation per state in a dict, track a state string, and reset the animation only when the state actually changes so you don't restart mid-loop every frame.

animated_sprite.py
class Hero(pygame.sprite.Sprite): def __init__(self, pos, anims): super().__init__() self.anims = anims # {'idle':Animation, 'run':..., 'jump':...} self.state = 'idle' self.facing = 1 # 1 right, -1 left self.image = self.anims[self.state].image() self.rect = self.image.get_rect(midbottom=pos) def set_state(self, s): if s != self.state: # only reset on a real change self.state = s self.anims[s].reset() def update(self, dt, keys): dx = keys[pygame.K_d] - keys[pygame.K_a] if dx: self.facing = dx self.set_state('run' if dx else 'idle') anim = self.anims[self.state] anim.update(dt) frame = anim.image() if self.facing < 0: frame = pygame.transform.flip(frame, True, False) self.image = frame # Group.draw uses self.image self.rect.x += dx * 4
Reset on change only.
If you call reset() every frame, the animation freezes on frame 0 forever. Switch and reset only when state truly changes — that's why set_state guards it.
One-shot states.
For non-looping animations (explosion, attack), set loop=False and check anim.done to return to idle or call self.kill() when the last frame finishes.
🎮 Build This: Concept / arcade polish — Animated Invaders

This is a concept/polish lesson that extends your Space Invaders (p5-7) — same shoot-em-up, now animated.

  1. Replace the static enemy image with a 2-frame sprite-sheet animation that pulses on a timer (independent of FPS).
  2. Build the reusable Animation class with accumulator-based frame stepping and looping control.
  3. Give the player an idle/thrust state pair, switching state from input via a guarded set_state.
  4. Add a non-looping explosion animation: spawn it when an enemy dies, and self.kill() the effect when anim.done.
  5. Flip frames for left/right facing instead of duplicating sheets, and confirm animation speed is identical at 30 and 120 FPS.
